content: Come with low expectations then enjoyed the movie even some scenes give "meh" or "too good to be true" kinda reaction but overall it's okay

summary: A Hilarious yet Flawed Union - Deadpool & Wolverine Review
content: The highly anticipated pairing of Deadpool and Wolverine has finally hit the screens, and while it delivers an abundance of laughter and action, it falls short of being a truly exceptional film due to some pacing and scripting issues.  One of the standout aspects of this movie is the return of Hugh Jackman as Wolverine, who slips back into the iconic role with ease. His presence is a welcome addition to the film, and his chemistry with Ryan Reynolds' Deadpool is undeniable. Reynolds, too, shines as the Merc with a Mouth, bringing his signature wit and irreverence to the role.  The opening scene sets the tone for the film, showcasing an impressive action sequence that seamlessly blends humor and intensity. This tone is maintained throughout the movie, with plenty of laugh-out-loud moments that will leave audiences entertained. However, some of these moments feel stretched or overly reliant on Reynolds' quick wit, which can become exhausting at times.  One of the primary issues with the film is its scripting. While the dialogue is often hilarious, some of Ryan Reynolds' lines feel too heavy-handed and could have been shortened or edited down for better flow. This is particularly noticeable in the film's second act, where the pacing begins to drag due to an overabundance of exposition and meandering subplots.  Despite these flaws, the film's strengths lie in its ability to balance humor and heart. The dynamic between Deadpool and Wolverine is genuinely engaging, and their banter is some of the film's highlights. The action sequences are also well-choreographed and visually stunning, making for an immersive viewing experience.  In conclusion, while Deadpool & Wolverine is an enjoyable film with plenty of laughs and exciting moments, it falls short of being a truly exceptional movie due to some scripting and pacing issues. With some tighter editing and more concise dialogue, this film could have reached its full potential. Nonetheless, fans of both characters will find plenty to love, and the chemistry between Jackman and Reynolds makes for a compelling watch.  Rating: 7/10  Recommendation: If you're a fan of either Deadpool or Wolverine, or enjoy action-comedies with a healthy dose of irreverence, this film is definitely worth watching. However, viewers seeking a more streamlined narrative may find themselves slightly disappointed.

The title of this might be misleading, but I did enjoy the film. It knows it's an action-packed superhero movie that you can turn your brain off to. There are lines of dialogue that literally allude to this when they exposition-dump on us or set up a fight sequence and it's this kind of meta humor that really makes it work.  There are plenty of funny moments, especially one involving a certain member of the X-Men. The buddy cop dynamic between Deadpool and Wolverine works well. It's got a ton of easter eggs for all the geeks who keep up with the lore, but unlike so many other recent Marvel projects, it doesn't feel like that's the only thing you have to look forward to while you watch it.  I only have two complaints: the fight scenes and the villains. I felt that the earlier Deadpool installments handled the action better. There were moments that made you scared for certain characters in the other movies, but that element was essentially missing from this one. After the first few they start to feel repetitive. There's one fight scene that somewhat compensates towards the film's conclusion, but overall I would've liked some more intensity from some of the others.  The villains felt lackluster. I won't say much because I don't want to spoil anything, but I would've preferred different ones. The ones we got weren't much of a spectacle and the salt in the wound is that the writers behind this are clearly capable of doing so.  It's 7/10 for me, go see it if you like Marvel and superheroes, but I don't think it's one of those superhero stories that can bring in folks outside of that demographic like Invincible.

summary: Fun But The First Two Are WAY Better
content: Now that the hype is finally died down on this film I think it's finally time that we talked more honestly about it. It's OK. Compared to the other two though it's not nearly as good, especially the second one. The thing that I liked about the first two Deadpool movies is that you could go into the theater and not know anything about Marvel and still enjoy it. Yes, there was 4th wall breaks, and there was also a ton of references towards other marvel films, but it was still coherent and it didn't make that the main focus. This time it feels like more of a requirement to watch other Fox Marvel, MCU Marvel, and even other Marvel properties to even understand what's going on. I watch those movies so I understood it and I definitely enjoyed it a lot, but this felt like more of a spoof movie this time than a dark action/comedy. I do really like the villains in this movie and that actress who plays the main one I really hope to see him more stuff because she is amazing. Also, yes, it is extremely funny, don't get me wrong. I had a really fun time. I laughed nonstop in the action scenes are incredible. I just wish the story was as good as the action and comedy. I enjoyed the references, and I love the fact that there was all actors who got the chance to reprise these rolls that they have played over the years... I just wish that they focused more on Deadpool's group of friends like the first two movies. The severe lack of Colossus and Teenage Warhead are definitely felt. I mean some of these references that they make in this movie are such deep cuts that you would have to be a part of the industry to be able to understand them. And from my understanding, some of those references are pretty mean spirited. But other than that, it's enjoyable and I do like how they wrap everything up and how they show love to more of the non-MCU marvel films at the end. It's OK. It's definitely not terrible. I just wish it felt more like a Deadpool movie and not an MCU movie.

summary: Its outstanding special effects can't elevate the picture past its poor storytelling and inconsistent tone.
content: The visuals are undoubtably fantastic, leading to some very unique action set-pieces that have enough style to set them apart from other superhero films, and its outstanding soundtrack is one of the best of the MCU. It's a shame, then, that the story and tone of 'Doctor Strange' drag it down to be one of the worst films of the Marvel cinematic universe. Stephen Strange is an unlikeable protagonist, one who we can never root for, and this means that the audience doesn't really have any reason to care about the story. Also, the narrative is very predictable and the film doesn't have a proper grasp on time; the 'training montage' is over very quickly and because there is no indication of how much time has passed, it seems as if Strange has gone from a nobody to the sorcerer supreme in mere days. Therefore, it feels entirely unbelievable that Strange can masterfully use his powers and save the world by the end of this film. Throughout the picture, Strange should have been struggling to use his powers, indicating to the audience that he is still learning and developing over time. Another big issue with the movie is its ham-fisted attempts at comedy. It is never funny, yet its 'humour' is always prevalent, even undermining serious moments on several occasions, and this becomes extremely annoying. Sure, a few comedic moments wouldn't have gone amiss, but since the character of 'Doctor Strange' isn't a funny one, this film should have had more of a focus on its serious elements and getting them to be successful. Without its superb visual elements and soundtrack, this could have been a disaster. 6/10

Benedict Cumberbatch, Rachel McAdams, Mads Mikkelson and Tilda Swinton star in this 2016 action film based on the Marvel Comics. Cumberbatch (Black Mass) plays the title role of Dr. Stephen Strange, a New York, neurosurgeon who gets into a car accident causing him nerve damage in both his hands. Soon, he heads to Tibet where he heals and learns from sorceress, The Ancient One (Swinton) about the mystical arts and tries to save the world from evil. McAdams (Wedding Crashers) plays Christine Palmer, Strange's ex-girlfriend/lover who is also a surgeon, Mikkelson (Casino Royale) plays evil sorcerer, Kaecilius and creator, Stan Lee makes a brief and amusing cameo as usual. This is a good comic book film, parts of it remind me of the "Star Wars" films and "Batman Begins," Cumberbatch is great in it, the rest of the cast is good as well and the visual/special effects are amazing. I recommend this.

summary: Quite Possibly The Most Authentically Psychedelic Movie You'll Ever See
content: Early on, during one of the first of many mind - and reality - bending moments in this film, it hit me that the 'Marvel Cinematic Universe' movies made the past 10 years are going to be seen in a half-century's time as just as classic and golden an age of film making as Disney's heyday in the 1940s, or at the very least, Pixar's rule of animation from the 90s on.  Yes, some are weaker than others - the second Thor film and the third Iron Man, for starters - but at their best they are very close to perfect, and so clearly what all those woefully misguided blockbuster abominations like The Force Awakens and Batman Vs Superman want to be. The elusive Marvel formula has been a fantastic balance of action, humour and heart, and this has all of those in spades - although if I have to pick any fault with it, it might be that the emotional charge is not as high as one might hope, and that's pretty much the only reason I took a star away. It was also apparent some PC coercion had influenced the casting and depictions of characters (white characters made black, male characters made female, and the only 'bad' or flawed characters in it being white males), but this was relatively restrained and unobtrusive and it would be churlish to kick up too much of a fuss over.  The most obvious comparison to the visual effects on display would be Inception, but I was struck while watching it, with my mouth open and a permanent smile of wonderment on my face, how this film technically surpasses everything in that film (and every other film I've seen too), but also how far more FUN it is than Inception.  So there we go, Dr Strange is a splendid, exciting, marvellously fun concoction that future generations are going to adore just as much as we do today. That's a rarity, and worth noticing while it happens.

This is my rating as a person who just watched Doctor Strange for the first time.  So, the movie was interesting and it grabbed my attention quickly. I loved the action scenes and the non-stop insanity. But I quickly came to realisation that this movie lacks in something.  Perhaps it lacks in characters. We see a total of 4 characters and some random ones who die the moment they step in front of Wanda. In addition to that, the movie lacks in... multiverses. Other worlds. I'd really love to see this movie with less action and more exploring.  Another problem is that the villain is more likeable than the good guys. I love Wanda. She's by far the strongest of them all and still she has emotion and she can feel pain.  Generally it is a good movie and it's worth watching especially in cinemas. The sound quality and CGI are god-tier.

As with the first "Doctor Strange" movie this is heavily dependent on visuals. It has been called a horror and though there are some horror aspects I wouldn't call it a horror as such. It is however probably the most visually violent.  It obviously opens up a lot of possibilities for the future of the franchise but I'll let you find out what they are by yourself.  There are some great introductions and cameos and lots of call backs to Sam Raimis previous movies.  It is still very much a superhero movie so don't go and expect a total departure from the rest of the MCU.

summary: A film that generated many expectations in general but they were not fully met.
content: After the premiere of Spider-Man: No Way Home, expectations were very high, fans began to create theories about the next Marvel movie to come and most were not pleased with the final result, including those who did not have expectations so high.  Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness is a movie that although it is very good at the beginning, and at a certain point the movie begins to decline.  Raimi's direction is something that comes through a lot in the film, which is a great thing, but sadly he didn't have full creative control.  The protagonist was overshadowed in his own film by the antagonist, and is ridiculed many times, which is normal in the MCU, but he did not expect that in a Raimi film.  Scarlet Witch was a good antagonist, even if her motivations are a bit misconstructed.  The overall acting was good, I think the best performance in this movie was from Elizabeth Olsen as Wanda Maximoff.  The cameos were wasted and only had about 5 minutes on screen, sadly.  The rhythm of this movie is very strange, in some parts it goes fast, in others it goes very slow and in others it is very boring.  The script uses a lot of easy ways out as conveniences (when Strange encourages America to use his powers correctly to defeat Wanda, thus gaining control of them out of nowhere) and a lot of very big holes.  The cinematography and visuals, I dare say, are among the best Marvel has ever had, Sam Raimi is an excellent director.  In general, this movie is good, I can't deny that, but although it has many strong points, it also has mistakes, and some that can make your experience in the movie not very pleasant.  In my personal opinion I give it a 7/10.

In 5,000 BC in Babylon, earthlings are attacked by the predator Deviants. The humanoid race of powerful Eternals are awaken in their spaceship by their creator Arishem and unleashed to destroy the Deviants with their abilities. However, they are not authorized to return to their ship and have to stay living on Earth along the centuries. On the present days, in London, the eternal Sersi (Gemma Chan) is walking near the river with her earthling boyfriend Dane Whitman (Kit Harington) and her friend and also eternal Sprite (Lia McHugh) when they are attacked by a Deviant. Soon the Eternals join themselves to fight the predator race that is more powerful now and they learn dreadful secrets about Arishem and their origins while fighting the predators.  "Eternals" is a good movie from Marvel Studios, with a long and good storyline about these superheroes Eternals. It is funny to see the concern with the representation of the minorities: the Latin Salma Hayek is Ajak; the Asians Gemma Chan and Ma Dong-seok are Sersi and Gilgamesh respectively; the deaf Lauren Ridloff is Makkari; Brian Tyree Henry is the black and gay Phastos. The conclusion informs that there will be a sequence in a near future. My vote is seven.  Title (Brazil): "Eternos" ("Eternals)

summary: Good movie, with easy to point out flaws
content: Eternals has so many materials to make a great mcu movie. The visuals and cinematography is breathtaking to watch, the action is pretty good, a lot of interesting lore and history dealing with the celestials and the universe in general, some great end credits scenes, good direction from Chloe zhao, and fantastic scenes of characters interacting either for comedy or drama (my favorite scene involves druig not being able to save people from genocide). However, it is not a perfect film. While all the actors do their best and all give great performances (standouts are kumail nanjiani, don lee, Brian tyree Henry, and Angelina Jolie for me) the characters are not given much development and as a result I care less for (there were some I did care for but not many), the villains suck, some actors are wasted (selma Hayek and bill skarsgard especially), so many emotional moments (except one with don and Angelina) I felt nothing for, tries to do way too much in one movie, and while some of the twists sound cool on paper in execution the motivations to why they happen are not developed well so they don't have the impact they should. Far from the worst mcu movie and not even close to the worst comic book movie ever made, just a pretty good film with some undeniable flaws.

summary: The way the narrative addresses various discussions such as the evolution of humanity, representation and diversity, makes the film very different from the most of the films
content: In the film, we follow a group of ten super-powered beings who came to Earth on a specific mission, given by the Celestial Arishem (voice of David Kaye): to end the threat of the Deviant monsters and, in the process, help humanity evolve without get involved in their conflicts. They arrive in the world at the dawn of human civilization and, over time, become immersed in the planet's culture, clinging to and mixing with their protÃ©gÃ©s.  The lack of tone change, rampant fanservice, and the immense need to connect everything in the MCU (Marvel Cinematic Universe) was generating a lot of friction between fans, producers and even movie lovers, raising some important questions, for example: is it still worth following this shared universe? And the movies/series need to be all connected or couldn't they be more independent?  Therefore, Eternals came with the promise of giving a greater freshness to this saturated universe, mainly by counting on ChloÃ© Zhao at the head of the project. The director and screenwriter won two Oscars for Nomadland, for best picture and best director. In fact, Kevin Feige, executive producer at Marvel, allowed Zhao to work with a lot of freedom. Despite still being a 'Marvel product', Eternals already positively differentiates itself by counting on the director's creative vision, which is wide open in all aspects of the film.  For this mixture of astronaut gods, Gnosticism and superheroes to work, Zhao chose to put the brakes on the action so that we could get to know each of the new protagonists in depth, how they relate to each other and how they see the world they live in - the which is essential for us to connect to the story. It just takes patience that the MCU has never demanded from its audience. And the big point is that fights every 10 minutes or that unbridled rush are not needed around here. It's a story about relationships and how that's the most human thing about us-even if it comes from near-divine beings. As much as the slower pace is very different from the formula that Marvel has presented us for almost a decade, the result is one of the most interesting stories in this universe.  The most important thing in Eternals is its characters. What makes the movie truly captivating is the dynamics of Marvel's gods. Setting up an origin story with several protagonists is no easy task, especially when no character is really known to the public. And the great merit of Eternals is to be able to present and develop each one of these immortal beings, dedicating a good part of the script so that we get to know, become affectionate and understand each member of the group, their personality and their way of seeing the world.  With a team of many heroes, Eternals has a large and diverse cast (perhaps the most varied in the history of the genre) and also irregular, which is normal when it comes to different personalities, ages and different genres. This is how we see Sersi's (Gemma Chan) passion for humanity, Kingo's (Kumail Nanjiani) narcissism and the Goblin's (Lia McHugh) frustration with her own nature. Each of the new heroes is portrayed with a unique personality that sets them apart from the rest, and they are all equally interesting and captivating. Everyone has their highlight moment, mixing well the typical Marvel humor with the particularities of each one. They all work well on their own and are even better together. Even Richard Madden's Ikaris is a great character, despite the actor's limited performance.  One of the main challenges of the script is to reconcile the wide range of characters - and the strong cast. Not just highlighting and developing each one, but making them relevant to the story. Does the film succeed? So-so. There are characters with little screen time and others appearing too much. Even more, you could feel the lack of depth in the central characters. Although the film presents interesting contexts and backgrounds for everyone, the personalities follow very simple archetypes, something that creates the impression that these heroes are just "more of the same". However, it doesn't necessarily mean something bad. Overall, the dynamic between the members works well. They have chemistry with each other and are charismatic most of the time.  And those character dynamics are key to making the whole story work. The real tension of the script isn't in how the group will defeat the Deviants or save the world, but how their different ways of looking at humanity and the mission they've been given clash - and for those conflicts to work, the people need to care about all ten eternals, which the movie does very well. But, as said, it takes time for all these relationships to be explored - and that's where Eternals hits the brakes on what we're used to seeing in the MCU. So, instead of having several action sequences that give the plot a celerity and a sense of urgency, the film is dedicated to showing the relationship between Ikaris and Sersi, to justify the mutual admiration of Gilgamesh (Don Lee) and Thena (Angelina Jolie) and explain why Druig (Barry Keoghan) disagrees with his brothers' methods.  The narrative is linear, but it alternates between the present day and important moments in human history, going back and forth in flashbacks. In this way, at the same time that we follow the trajectory of the heroes in getting together, we see what led them to be in this situation. Definitely, the film breaks several standards set by Marvel. It cares a lot more about telling a story than inflating the MCU even more with references and warnings about upcoming movies.  However, the influence of Marvel is still present, unfortunately in a negative way. There are characters needlessly out of tune, which only serve to bring comic relief. Not that it's bad, most of the funny scenes work, but it totally breaks the rhythm and many are no longer taken seriously. Furthermore, there are many inconsistencies that are difficult to swallow, especially involving the reasons why Deviants exist. The impression left at the end is that the alien beings only have the purpose of composing action scenes.  Yes, there are some problems in "Eternals", such as digital effects that are not impressive, some arms of the story that are not that interesting, secondary characters that don't arouse any feelings, a semi-villain poorly explored and, above all, a feeling that that story doesn't take place in the Marvel movie universe. Despite the script having some notable holes, and the film's duration being excessively long, it still delivers a good, engaging story that brings a freshness that Marvel so badly needed. Thanks to ChloÃ© Zhao, Eternals is one of the films with the most personality in this universe. It is remarkable how great the direction of scenes and actors are. The director brings her naturalistic perspective to filming, valuing every detail to the fullest. All the combat choreography is very well done, taking advantage of both the abilities of each of the eternals and the visual impact they offer to compose scenes that are really impactful.  With stunning scenes filmed in real locations, Eternals is a very beautiful film both in terms of looks and in the message of union and love that it conveys to viewers. The soundtrack is noteworthy and completes the epic setting, emphasizing emotional moments and punctuating other scenes to amplify the effect of what is happening on screen. Another peculiar point is that, despite the soberer and contemplative tone than we are used to in the MCU, the long is not afraid to make references either to other Disney franchises or even to characters from the "competitor" DC Comics, responding in the can to fan-asked questions like "Why didn't the Eternals help in the fight against Thanos" and more. It's interesting to see a direct, honest and wide-open answer on the big screen.  In addition, the way the narrative addresses various discussions such as the evolution of humanity, representation and diversity, makes the film very different from more banal productions that lead nowhere - as is the case with several feature films of the genre. Although Eternals still has several slips, it delivers a solid story that works very well for making us care about those illustrious strangers. The whole dynamic of these gods is what sustains the plot and makes the twists and conflicts have the necessary weight both to impact us and to design what is to come in the future of the cinematic universe.  It was a tremendous success to bring someone like ChloÃ© Zhao, who brought identity, body and soul to a project that had everything to go wrong. Even with Marvel intervening to connect the film to its cinematic universe, the director and screenwriter's vision was essential for us to have a good story, without having to get so attached to the MCU. Now, it remains to be seen if Marvel will continue to bring in Zhao and other professionals as good as they do because that's exactly what their films need, directors who bring personality traits to their films. The shadow created by Avengers: Endgame is still a problem for this Phase 4. The public's expectation and anxiety for something as great as a monster that Marvel itself created and that now seems to struggle to tame. However, audiences are sure to find Eternals one of the most interesting things the MCU has offered in a long time.

summary: Weak script, lazy direction or poor characters?
content: As most of the audience out there, I enjoy Marvel films. They're cleverly written to please the viewer a brain-resting title that makes you feel accomplished, part of something bigger and, most of all, doesn't make you feel uncomfortable. I wasn't dying to watch Guardians of the Galaxy for two reasons: I didn't know about that specific Marvel group of heroes and the marketing image built around it gave me the feeling that it wasn't for me. Something changed, several people made positive comments about it therefore I watched it. I was pretty excited at the idea of another brain-resting enjoyable marvel film (you know where I'm going here). The all film was a disappointment. Chris Pratt didn't get the sympathy he was desperately looking for with his melancholic mix tape. Rocket the raccoon tried to lift the humor of the film with few quite amusing episodes, but overall he also didn't make me want to care. Neytiri.. oh no, I meant... Gamora was cold an distant from the story itself. I felt I spent two hours watching at the desperate attempts of the cast of getting my attention, when I could really only focus and enjoy on Vin Diesel's brilliant performance. I don't know if it was a poor script or if James Gunn didn't feel as inspired with this cast as with Sarah Michelle Gellar and friends. Maybe it's just the fact that not all the characters from the Marvel jar of Superheroes can be turned into cinema. I was disappointed, therefore I will a bit more doubt the Marvel productions coming up next.

I finished this film, thinking, "There are people out there suffering and I can do something to help them out." This is a story about a place most people might not be able to conceive: where things are dying, where people survive off liquor and stale food, where those who are supposed to love us drive knives into our backs. There are fisticuffs back and forth with those who claim to work for justice. The film is dark, brooding, intense and most of all depressing. Great performances help to enhance this amazing story of friendship and violence. It's an amazing work and everything I had hoped for. A masterful work that imbues you to it through its realism.

summary: A fun and fresh space adventure
content: "Guardians of the Galaxy," directed by James Gunn, is a refreshing addition to the Marvel Cinematic Universe, blending humor, action, and a quirky ensemble of characters. The film follows Peter Quill (Chris Pratt), who is abducted from Earth as a child and grows up to become the space-faring rogue Star-Lord. He forms an unlikely alliance with a group of misfits: the fierce Gamora (Zoe Saldana), the literal Drax the Destroyer (Dave Bautista), the wisecracking Rocket Raccoon (voiced by Bradley Cooper), and the lovable tree-like Groot (voiced by Vin Diesel).  The movie's charm lies in its offbeat humor and the chemistry among its main characters. The soundtrack, filled with classic '70s and '80s hits, adds a nostalgic and energetic vibe that complements the film's tone. The visual effects are stunning, bringing the vibrant and diverse galaxy to life.  While the plot revolves around a familiar battle to save the universe from a powerful villain, it's the character development and their camaraderie that make the film stand out. Each member of the Guardians brings their own backstory and personality, creating a dynamic that is both entertaining and heartfelt.  "Guardians of the Galaxy" successfully blends action, comedy, and heart, making it an enjoyable and unique entry in the superhero genre.

First, don't get me wrong. It was definitely a visually stunning movie with the same light-hearted characters we all came to love in Volume 1. However, things all seemed a little forced and cheesy in the second volume. I thought, personally, there could have been a little bit more action and less expository between the characters. I am all about character development, but every scene seemed to be another sad sob story with another one of the characters. Also, in the end, it never really seemed like the universe ever needed saving.

It is a really entertaining movie, but it just can not live up to its predecessor. Missing the "wow" factor the producers decided to give too much from the essentials of vol. 1. Drax repeats the same jokes, but now twice, repeatedly. Dancing in action, now by baby Groot, but for several minutes. Racoon jokes become also a bit flat after a while. Family issues are also too much. It is discussed by several times by Yondu, Star Lord, Ego, Drax, Gamora and Nebula...etc. Too long, too much sometimes (Yondu funeral), and it resulted in not being as fresh and vivid as vol. 1 is.

summary: Not as good as the first but still very enjoyable
content: Yeah, I said what I had to say in the subject line. I have a question about this movie. After the Guardians ship crash lands on Berhart, (prob spelling that wrong) Quill and Gamora have a private talk with one another where Gamora brings up the David Hasselhoff story that Quill told her someone before when he was drunk. The two were by themselves with nobody around at all. HOWEVER, later on in the movie when Quill realizes that Ego killed his Mother and knows what Ego's plan is, he attacks Ego. When Ego recovers, he changes his "human form" to David Hasselhoff. How the hell did he know to change himself to look like the Hoff!?!?!? This means that he somehow heard the private conversation between Quill and Gamora or just blindly guessed; and I HIGHLY DOUBT that Ego just guessed that. It's a small nit-pick but it does kinda bother me lol. Btw, does anybody have an explanation for this???  Also, when Quill, Gamora and Drax are about to go with Ego and Mantis to his planet (aka himself? lol) Rocket is seen spraying the wreckage of the ship with this gun looking thing and the all the wires and metal are just randomly reappearing! Rocket is creating matter out of NOTHING! WTF is that!?!?!? That's insane! Am I the ONLY one fascinated with this???... and the fact that he only uses it the one time and for nothing else is a waste. Can it just repair machinery that wad already there or can he just make any kind of machinery just randomly show up or off thin air??? I believe that even Tony Stark and Dr. Strange would be impressed with that technology/magic! Would that be considered tech or would that be magic? lol... idk but either way it's an impressive gadget that we see Rocket using for only a handful of seconds and then it disappears FOREVER! lol

summary: An okay movie that just isn't made for me.
content: The performances are good, with some funny and heartfelt moments in there, my favorite being Drax become friends and becoming a parent to the kids trapped on the spaceship. This is also mostly a rocket centered story, with the team trying to save rocket while battling bad guys who want t rocket. The villain is fine, played by Chudwudi Iwuji with his motive being want to change this he doesn't like.  Finally, the visual effects for which this film was nominated for they're alright. I personally don't think they should win, but I can see why The Academy were nominated since they were a treat to look at and also Disney probably paid a lot of money toward the for your consideration campaign.  Guardians of the Galaxy Vol 3 is a funny but finer movie that I think teenagers and Marvel fans will enjoy, but personally it's not my cup of tea and also left me wanting more.

There were a lot of hype about this movie so of course i expected a lot from this. I got exactly what i was hoping for: It was funny but some times serious. The flash backs got me going and they were perfectly timed. The acting was great while also having good dialog. The story was really spot on and the charme of the characters hit me with success. My only complains is Adam Warlock: He is the most pointless person to ever exist. Is he made to be funny? Cause hes not! Trash dialog and weird character. WHY? Also the planet counter-Earth was just stupid. Also the High Evolutionaries team was boring. They should have made them cold ass if they were to be part of it. Marvel is lacking coldness unless for High Evolutionary of course. Last complain is Kraglin and Cosmo. No reason for them to be there. Only good scene with them is the Yondu scene.

This movie is generally satisfying, but it's long, it takes quite a while to get going and parts of it seem forced. In short, IMHO it's by far the weakest of the three though still better than anything Marvel has put out since the last Spiderman (which was actually a Sony movie).  The first portion of the movie seemed forced and didn't flow very well. The dialog was pretty weak, the jokes didn't land particularly well, and it jumps all over the place. I was bored and wondered what all the fuss was about. After that, the pace picks up and I started enjoying it.  Good news. Disney's woke BS is minimal, and very subtle when it does rear its head. The acting is first rate across the board, though I think Gunn could've been a bit more convincing than he was. The antagonist nails it.  The bad news--it's definitely longer than it needs to be. Standard for today's Hollywood which seems to have forgotten the beauty of brevity. And it didn't know when to end, which should've been right after the final confrontation. But of course, the MCU has to try and set up characters for spin-offs. Stupid mouse.  In the end, it's definitely far more somber in tone than the previous two, and if you're easily upset by animal cruelty, you might want to skip it.  That said, I enjoyed it and might watch it again in the future. Not in the near future however. I'd rather watch the first one again.

summary: "So you're a man who has everything,..., and nothing."
content: So here I am in my late fifties with enough of a connection to my comic book collecting past to be intrigued by modern day super hero films. Now I was never a Marvel Comics geek, my favorite characters were the mainstays of the DC Comics universe, like Superman, Batman, Green Lantern, et al. But if you were a fan, you couldn't help but know about the alternative universe that contained Spider-Man, The Incredible Hulk, The X-Men and Iron Man, which brings us to the subject at hand. I thought that the movie did a credible job of spanning the forty year comic book history of Iron Man about as well as could be expected in a distilled down two hour movie. Particularly effective was the way we see how the origin costume in the picture pays tribute to the early Iron Man years, while the modern day red and gold armor conveys the advance of science and technology into the twenty first century. Comic book purists will most likely have serious issues with the picture, but I've been able to get over the whole faithfulness to continuity issue some time ago. What I'm looking for in a flick like "Iron Man" is an opportunity to be entertained for a couple of hours, and there's enough here to have some fun and be just that - entertained.  The one thing I AM looking for in the Marvel universe pictures is the ubiquitous cameo appearance of Stan Lee, creator of many of the comic book icons we recognize today. He's incorrectly credited in the cast listing as I write this, as he's listed appearing as himself. What you need to do is pay attention to that party scene in the casino when Tony Stark says hi to 'Hef'. That was just the coolest way to get Stan into the picture, and I give him a lot of credit for not taking himself too seriously as he truly is one of the gods of comic book history.  As for the picture, it's easy enough to bash it for it's adherence to current world events and the milieu of extremist terrorism. But is that any worse than reliving the events of the early 1960's and placing the character in the middle of the Bay of Pigs or the Cuban Missile Crisis? The present day Iron Man can be a product of his time and still be effective as a symbol of patriotism and honor, even while the rest of the country is immersed in presidential primaries and partisan politics.  You have to give credit to the selection of Robert Downey Jr. for the Iron Man role. Perhaps his own bouts with substance abuse uniquely prepared him for an outstanding performance as the conflicted title character. However the character I was intrigued with the most was Jeff Bridges' Obadiah Stane, I just couldn't get over his appearance as Tony Stark's operations chief. The role was played with just the right amount of reserve and just below the surface malice; I thought he did an excellent job. Gwyneth Paltrow had pretty much a thankless job as Stark's personal assistant and secret admirer and it didn't seem to me that her performance was all that stand out.  As an aside, the one thing I feel I do have to comment on is a personal gripe of mine relative to the IMDb ranking system. As I write this, "Iron Man" sits at number #130 of the all time best films as ranked by current users. It seems to me that the way fans line up behind a picture even before it's released, there ought to be a moratorium of say, two or three years before a new release could be eligible for Top 250 ranking. When you consider the truly great films of all time like "Casablanca" or "Citizen Kane", it's hard for me to reconcile a popcorn flick like this one to be considered in the same breath. I feel the same way (as to a voting moratorium) about stunning pictures like "No Country For Old Men" and "There Will Be Blood". If a picture has the legs to stand up as an all time great, it will be remembered a couple years after it's original release. Remember all the fanboy hype about "Serenity"? Does anyone even care about that picture today? See what I mean?

4 May 2008. A step-above the normal superhero, in fact, this movie is more spoiled rich man with technology who discovers a conscience. This movie movie delivers a Robocop (1987) except bigger and larger but without the more poignant loss of Robocop's wife and family, delivers the Aliens (1986) scene of hi-tech individual warfare with the alien factor that actually provides a more intimate Civil War (1861-1864) angle, and Terminator 2: Judgment Day (1991) without the alien factor again. What Iron Man can't provide is due to its very nature - the everyman, common man perspective, a level that the mass audience can identify with as with the Spiderman franchise as well as the more personal relational boy-girl conflict. While Iron Man is truly intellectual with a conscience, that is what it remains whereas Spiderman focuses on the universal experience of all of us. Additional, unnecessary flashback sequence like with Batman Begins (2005) or even the more contemporary movie such as Made of Honor (2008) allows for the underdevelopment of the familial relationships important to the movie. So while Iron Man is worth its ticket, it allows some of the gloss to overlook the more important flash underneath. Seven out of Ten Stars.

Entertaining, but not nearly as good as advertised. Robert Downey Jr. is more fun to watch as Tony Stark than Peter Paker in SpiderMan, or Batman for that matter, if only because he's not the tormented hero struggling with his conscious, he's a billionaire playboy who even when escaping with his life is enjoying building himself a high tech cyborg armor. Pathos is replaced with on screen charisma and witty snappy dialog, and all actors play there parts well here. My only problem was that while Stark get's plenty of screen time, I feel like Iron Man only showed up for the last half hour of the film, which I guess is common enough in this origin stories, but still made the whole thing feel kind of uneven to me. I get the impression if the director, actors, writers stay together for the next film they might have ironed out the bugs. (SPOILER)The next film could be the super-hero jamboree "The Avengers", as indicated by Samuel L. Jackson as Nick Fury, at the end of the credits. And this character might do better in a cross over. Like Spider Man and Batman this film is pretty close to how I always envisioned the character, there's just not enough action and hero screen time here. Still it's a super-hero movie for people who don't necessarily care for them, and that's saying a lot. So enjoyable, not great, but good.
Iron man 2 is a average movie unlike its predecessor which was establishing a great superhero franchise.

summary: It's not about us. It's about legacy.
content: After the release of Iron Man, people were ready for another type of superhero genre, not goofy and campy but also not serious and brooding. Iron Man helped this into Hollywood and the world, so Iron Man 2 had to continue the run and prove that it can do the same things but still be a good movie. And boy, did it...not.  It has the good elements of Iron Man: RDJ's performance, humor and entertaining action. However, there are the negatives in this movie too. The villain is not even different from the first Iron Man. Sure, it is technically two people this time with Justin Hammer and Ivan Vanko, but their personalities are the same as Obadiah Stane. Hammer is a billionaire weapons developer who wants Tony's tech and tries to replicate it. Vanko is a vindictive man with ties to Tony's father, Howard, who fights Tony in the end with a much larger suit of armor. So no real difference except that it was split into two people. There is also the fact that it tries so hard to set up so much, such as the Avengers, introducing Shield, War Machine and Black Widow. That is a lot to cover and it doesn't leave time for a good story.  RDJ continues to be great as Tony Stark. I like that they explored his alcohol problem as it just looks further into his insecurities. There was also the scene where Tony watches a prerecorded video by his father, Howard, saying that his son is his greatest creation. To me that was pretty powerful. Kudos to John Slattery for acting like a tool like Tony, but still being able to loving and adoring of his son.   The humor in this movie is also very good, especially for RDJ. The Senate committee hearing is really funny as Tony is just blowing every question off and acting entitled, which isn't unusual for him but RDJ makes it look so easy and it let's us like Stark even more.   The action too is very good, including the fight in Monaco, Rhodey v Stark and the final battle against the Hammer Drones. I will say that the sound effects used for the suits moving was very distracting as it was so clear how much CGI was used. The suits used in this movie were really incredible such as the suitcase suit, War Machine's suit and Tony's final suit.  In this movie, there is so much they have to set up for future movies, mostly the Avengers. It very clearly gets in the way of the story and while it is good to know what could be, it was very time consuming.  I will also say that the product placement in this film was insane such as Oracle, Elon Musk's cameo, and all the other products shown at the Stark Expo.  While it is an entertaining movie, there is so much that gets in the way that really distracts from the movie itself.  + RDJ + Cool, new suits + Fun action scenes and humor - Rehashed villains - Too much setup for future  Final Score: 7.2/10

This movie is a complete mess, but it's so enjoyable that I love it anyway. All the performances are entertaining as hell, the humor is on point, and visually this movie is a treat. The main issue is that it just has too many plots happening simultaneously, making it feel more disjointed. As for the characters, Tony continues to be just the worst (yet so lovable). He continues his path of taking responsibility for his actions and grappling with his legacy, and that's of his father's. As for Rhodey, I do prefer Don Cheadle over the previous actor. His banter with Tony is just so fun. Fury and Coulson are great as always here. Black Widow makes her first appearance, and while I think they went a bit overboard in sexualizing her, she's shown to be easily the most competent one in the cast, so I'm not too upset about it. Justin Hammer is also a joy, and I'm so sad that he didn't get more time to shine. Overall this one is just a great time in my opinion.
It would have been better if Jon Favreau directed it

summary: Very entertaining, but the worst Iron Man movie
content: After The Avengers there was a lot of hype for the next Marvel movie, and what better way to live up to the hype than with an Iron Man movie. On the first watch its very entertaining, and exciting but after watching it other times, you start to see a lot of problems. The story is very forgetable, just as the second movie but this time, the new things they try to add are or bad, or completly deleated, Pepper having powers, the PTSD of Tony, the little kid tony meets, Iron Patriot, the evolution of Tony saying he will do other things besides being Iron Man, etc. All those new things are worthless since they are only in this movie, plus the forgettable story with a really bad and weak villain with no motivations makes this movie almost a skip when watching all the Marvel movies. Also, this is I think the first time where some jokes are leading the story, the story of the mandarin seemed really interesting and he looked very menacing, but it was just ruined for the sake of throwing some unexpected jokes. But still with those problems, its very exciting to watch, it has some of the best moments of Iron Man and very cool scenes which ultimately make the story worthy.

Iron Man 3 Is Good With A Slightly Darker Tone Than Iron Man (2008) And Iron Man 2 (2010). I Don't Love Its Action Sequences But I Very Much Enjoy The Plot Of The Film And Think It Is Entertaining. It Wasn't As Good As Iron Man Although It Was An Improvement From The 2nd One.  I Would Give Iron Man 3 A 7.2/10.

I can tell you the reason to see this film is solely based on the performance of the incredible Hong Kong actor Tony Leung as Shang-Chi's father Wenwu aka the real MANDARIN. He literally carried this film on his back. For those who aren't film buffs, Tony Leung is basically Leonardo DiCaprio, Tom Hanks, Denzel Washington and Brad Pitt combined in Hong Kong. Even though Hollywood has knocked on his door a few times, finally, better late than never, he gets his due in Hollywood.  Simu Liu throughout most of the film, I thought was alright, nothing too special, nothing too terrible. However, he has two incredible moments in the film where then I was completely sold on his character. A great display of acting, one of which involves a scene with Tony Leung where he calls out his failure as a parent. What I also admired is that Shang-Chi also has blood on his hands. He reveals this to Awkwafina's Katy and Simu doesn't overdo his performance in that scene. You felt his anger, his guilt and his remorse. This reveal reminded me he almost killed the Death Dealer, seeing and knowing this makes his character a bit more rich and complex. I appreciated that he wasn't a saint.  The relationship between Shang-Chi and his father Wenwu on the surface does involve superhero theatrics, however underneath it, it does have a very relatable dynamic. As an Asian myself, I think it will hit home to most Asians and their relationship with their parents.  But despite all that, I did not love the film like I wanted to. It still falls under the same old formulaic humor I despise, mainly coming from Awkwafina. I will however concede that I did expect the humor to be a lot worse and thankfully it isn't too unbearable. But if it were up to me, "less is more." I had some inkling that Sir Ben Kingsley would show up and he does add the redundant humor into the mix.  Although it works on some level, the story mainly lumbers along, obsessed with backstory. On the one hand I did feel the drag, but in the other I felt the flashbacks did help flesh out Wenwu, Shang-Chi and his sister Xialing whom Meng'er Zhang does a good job portraying. Fala Chen also had a great supporting role as Shang-Chi's mother.  While the creatures showcased in final act are pretty cool like the Great Protector, I particularly disliked Morris the faceless chicken-pig that accompanied Ben Kingsley's Trevor Slattery and found the addition very unnecessary. Speaking of unnecessary Florian Munteanu's Razor Fist was not needed. I felt all his screen time could have been devoted to the Death Dealer who I was more interested in.  I also did feel like they took some tropes and traditions of Asian cinema and did not present anything unique on their own take and in doing so I got this feeling that at least half of the film did not know what it wanted to be other than an Asian American hero in the MCU.  To sum it up, a stellar first half, where then Marvel eventually goes back into their comfort zone by the third act. Despite Tony Leung's magic as a performer and Simu Liu's best efforts it is ultimately another okay entry to the MCU as opposed to the top tier level films that took big swings like INFINITY WAR, the first IRON MAN, the CAPTAIN AMERICA trilogy or the first GUARDIANS. Again, being an Asian myself I wanted to love this film, but I didn't, I just thought it was okay.  Two post credits scenes so stay put.  7/10.
